data.title: Nvidia And Intel: Beauty And The Beast Team Up
data.description:
Well folks, it's a Thursday morning bombshell as markets open in the wake of announcements from Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) that the companies have inked a partnership to package the former's graphics chips with the latter's CPUs in processors that sport Nvidia's proprietary NVLink tech. As part of the deal, Nvidia will also take a $5 billion equity stake in Intel, which has sent INTC soaring, up 25% as I write this. This is a major upward catalyst for Intel at a time when the company was in dire need of a win, and for Nvidia this gives it easy access to x86 CPUs and provides additional rapport with the U.S. government.

I don't think this is a match many saw coming, but it certainly presents interesting synergies between the two chipmakers and has opened up new possibilities at a time when AI is pushing the processor sector to rapidly evolve. I think it'd be useful to break this partnership down by looking at how it benefits each company. Let's start with Intel.

It's no secret that Intel has been struggling to deliver on turnaround promises as its foundry continues to languish without external customers and its processors fall behind Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) in performance and power efficiency. The company has pinned a lot of its prospects on the success of its 18A manufacturing node, but has seen mixed results and is already pushing some resources towards the development of next-generation 14A instead. In the meantime, AMD has been using TSMC's (TSM) latest nodes to great effect, stealing market share, and especially revenue share, away from Intel. What better way to turn things around than to team up with the most prolific processor seller of our time.

In the enterprise space (i.e., data center), Intel has agreed to provide custom CPUs that will be packaged up with Nvidia's leading-edge AI accelerator GPUs and connected using NVLink to provide fast and efficient chips in a space that Intel has been significant losing ground. The data center market offers higher margins and higher volumes, which Intel desperately needs as its foundry business continues to bleed cash flow.

In the consumer market, Nvidia has agreed to package GPU chiplets into Intel x86 CPUs to offer an integrated SOC for PC applications, which is another area where AMD has been eating Intel's lunch as the former's Zen 5 and 3D V-Cache enabled CPUs have dominated the market in recent years.

Lastly of course, the $5 billion equity investment is no small gesture as it amounts to around 4% of the company. The purchase is subject to regulatory approval of course, but considering the U.S. government is now a significant owner of Intel stock, it seems highly unlikely that any regulatory hurdles would pose an issue.

Which leads nicely into the next topic of discussion, the pros of this partnership for Nvidia. As the most valuable company in the US, the overall dollar effect of this deal will have a substantially lower impact than it does for Intel, but it still provides some interesting benefits. The one I consider to be the most important is that it makes Nvidia and the U.S. government bedfellows at a time when the Trump administration seems poised to take a more active role in the economy and the chipmaking sector in general in the interest of national security.

While the U.S. government paid around $20 for its 10% stake in INTC, Nvidia is paying around $23 per share, which, along with 25%+ jump on Thursday, has set up an attractive cost basis and will score some political points with White House. Following the initial catastrophe of the tariff war and the Trump administration's threats to impose semiconductor tariffs on Taiwan and other countries, it's good business for the company to keep ties amicable any way it can. A joint investment in an entity that is vital to the U.S. national interest is a savvy move in that context.

The partnership also brings Nvidia closer to the U.S. following China essentially banning its domestic companies from purchasing Nvidia's H20 GPUs in order to encourage the use of homegrown AI accelerators. As the AI arms race escalates, the political aspect of operating in the market becomes more significant and with this Intel deal, Nvidia is positioning itself masterfully, in my opinion, to continue to reap the benefits.

As another benefit, packaging Intel CPUs with its AI chips, gives Nvidia a foothold in the eternal and undying x86 market, which has proven hard to break into as the Intel and AMD duopoly has posed significant barriers to entry. As Intel has been attempting to develop and refine its own GPU offerings, these barriers have remained, but it appears with this deal that Intel might be admitting defeat on that front and now sees more long-term benefit from allowing Nvidia in and collaborating than in competing.

The integration of Nvidia GPUs and Intel CPUs into a single SOC connected by NVLink has the potential to pose a threat to AMD as these chips will likely have low latency and power efficiencies that are impossible when the components are sold and packaged discretely.

Despite these synergies and benefits to both companies, there are some unanswered questions here. First, the deal makes no indication that these chips will be manufactured at Intel's foundry (though the CPU part presumably would, assuming 18A is up to snuff) and without an external customer it remains uncertain whether the foundry will survive. Will these chips be manufactured using TSMC? Even if that might be in Intel's and Nvidia's best interest, the downside of being a joint partner with the U.S. government is that the foundry will likely get priority despite its current status as a money pit.

Second, while developing chips together is all well and good and is an indicator of future collaboration, the actual volume that will result from this deal remains undetermined. Will this truly be Nvidia and Intel's flagship offerings in the x86 space? Will demand for these chips materialize? Will 18A (and eventually 14A) reach yields where mass production is even feasible? And how will Intel balance the interests of profitability alongside the U.S. government's interests that the foundry remain operational by any means necessary?

It's certainly a messy web, but overall I think this partnership results in more benefits than problems for both companies.

Investor Takeaway

Despite some uncertainties, I think this partnership has the potential to truly ignite Intel's turnaround plans as it now has major partners with deep pockets invested in its success. I have advocated for a long time that Intel should keep its foundry and rebuild through manufacturing success, and while it's not exactly the path I envisioned, the company appears to be on a favorable trajectory in this vein. I continue to see INTC as a medium-risk, high-reward play and am re-iterating a Buy even as the stock has risen 50% since my last article.

For Nvidia, I think this deal provides insulation from a lot of the political whirlwind that the company has been caught in multiple times over the past year -- becoming a joint partner with the U.S. government in a sector that has proven important to the President's agenda for domestic manufacturing for just a $5 billion investment is a stroke of genius, especially when Nvidia has so much cash to spend.

Besides being in the good graces of the U.S. government, this partnership gives Nvidia more leverage in the lucrative x86 market and provides another potential avenue for growth after China has turned off the spigot. Despite valuation concerns, NVDA has proven resilient and the AI market has continued to roll right along as GPUs remain the hottest commodity around. However, with AI reaching a fever pitch, I think investors should wait for a better entry point on the stock so, even with the pros of this Intel-Nvidia partnership, I am rating NVDA a Hold.

Thanks for reading!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Apogee Enterprises: Even In Light Of Recent Weaknesses, It Will Be Fine
data.description:
Sometimes, even when bullish calls do work out, they take a while to do so. Sometimes, you can expect to underperform the market for a time. A good example of one of my calls that has recently been underperforming is Apogee Enterprises (NASDAQ:APOG), a business that produces and sells windows, curtain walls, high-performance glass, and other similar offerings, with a focus on the non-residential space. Back in May of this year, I ended up reaffirming the company as a "Buy" candidate. Even though revenue, profits, and cash flows, were all showing signs of weakness, I believed that the long-term picture would be positive. Shares of the business were also attractively priced on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar companies. But since then, the stock is up only 6.7%. That's barely more than half the 11.8% rise that the broader market experienced during this time.

Lately, fundamental performance has been mixed, with top-line results improving, but bottom-line results worsening. The good news is that management expects revenue to improve this year. But adjusted net profits are still forecasted to decline. To be honest with you, I believe that further weakening is possible. But in the long run, I firmly believe that the company will do just fine. And with how the stock is valued on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms, I think that maintaining it as a "Buy" candidate is the right move.

Still bullish

Lately, Apogee Enterprises has faced some pain. For its most recent quarter, which is its first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, the business reported sales of $346.6 million, which represents an increase of 4.6% compared to the $331.5 million reported one year earlier. According to management, this rise in revenue was driven by a $22 million benefit that the company got from its acquisition of UW Solutions, which management acquired in November of last year in exchange for $240.9 million in cash. But on a net basis, cost shareholders $232.2 million. Without this, revenue would have actually dropped year over year.

When it comes to the different operating segments of the company, the real driver was the firm’s Performance Surfaces segment, which saw revenue shoot up 99.3% from $21.2 million to $42.3 million. But again, this was because of the aforementioned acquisition. Actual organic revenue dipped by $1 million because of unfavorable product mix and broader market weaknesses. Other operating segments for the company largely weakened as well. The one exception to this was the Architectural Services segment, which reported a 7.6% increase in revenue from $99 million to $106.5 million. According to management, the segment benefited from higher volumes. But beyond that, there really wasn't much in the way of details.

The Architectural Metals segment, meanwhile, reported a 3.4% drop in revenue. When you look at the data provided, this was because of organic weakness, with revenue contracting from $133.2 million to $128.6 million. A less favorable product mix more than offset higher volumes. And finally, the Architectural Glass segment for the company plunged 15.5%, from $86.7 million to $73.3 million. This was driven entirely by weakness on the organic side, with management blaming it on lower end market demand pushing volumes down.

Profitability for the company worsened during this time. Net profits, for instance, went from $31 million to negative $2.7 million. One pain point here ended up being interest expense, which grew from $0.5 million to $3.8 million. But the biggest issue, by far, was a jump in its cost of sales from $232.7 million to $271.5 million. The company did experience during this time certain acquisition-related costs of about $7 million, as well as a small amount of restructuring expenses. But higher aluminum costs, high tariff expenses caused by the trade war situation that's going on, and an unfavorable product mix all worked against the company. It didn't help that restructuring charges of $8.4 million also negatively affected selling, general, and administrative expenses.

The good news is that adjusted net profits did come in stronger than GAAP net profits. But they still fell heartily from $31.9 million to $11.9 million. Other profitability metrics for the business also worsened. As an example, operating cash flow fell from $5.5 million to negative $19.8 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a worsening from $49.1 million to $27.3 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company dropped from $52.6 million to $34.4 million.

When it comes to the rest of this year, the picture does seem to be rather mixed as well. Because of the aforementioned acquisition, as well as strength regarding the Architectural Services segment, revenue is expected to come in at between $1.40 billion and $1.44 billion. That would be above, even at the low end, the $1.36 billion the company reported for last year. On the other hand, adjusted earnings per share are forecasted to come in at between $3.80 and $4.20. If the firm hits the midpoint here, that would mean adjusted net income of $85.4 million. That compares negatively to the $108.9 million the company reported the previous year. And if we continue to track declines like we have when it comes to other profitability metrics, adjusted operating cash flow would be around $129.2 million, while EBITDA would total $151.1 million.

In the event that these estimates come to fruition, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. Even with the worsening expected for this year on the bottom line, shares of the company look attractively priced on an absolute basis. Furthermore, relative to other similar enterprises, shares are trading on the cheap as well. In the table below, I compared it to five such enterprises. On a price-to-earnings basis and on a price-to-operating cash-flow basis, I found that it ended up being the cheapest of the group. And when it comes down to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, only one of the companies that I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Apogee Enterprises 11.0 7.3 8.1
American Woodmark Corporation (AMWD) 12.2 10.2 7.9
Insteel Industries (IIIN) 24.7 12.8 12.0
Quanex Building Products Corporation (NX) 33.3 8.6 8.2
JELD-WEN Holding (JELD) 58.6 33.3 11.7
Janus International Group (JBI) 33.6 7.6 11.8

Near term, as the economy weakens, we could see some further pain for shareholders. Having said that, I do believe in the long-term outlook for the company. Some of this optimism centers around continued strategic actions the company is taking to boost results in the long run. In the final quarter of last year, for instance, management completed Project Fortify, which was a restructuring initiative aimed at streamlining resources in its Architectural Services and corporate/other operations. That ended up costing shareholders $16.7 million but led to annualized pre-tax cost savings of about $14 million.

And in late April of this year, management announced a second phase of this that should boost results for not only the Architectural Services segment but also the Architectural Metals segment. This plan is expected to cost shareholders somewhere between $24 million and $26 million. A lot of this will involve severance and employee-related costs as management reduces headcount. But when everything is said and done, management anticipates annualized savings of between $13 million and $15 million.

There's also the fact that the space that the company plays in is incredibly large. And, it should continue to grow over time. For instance, management estimates that the total U.S. non-residential construction space is worth around $575 billion a year. The much smaller US glazing market that includes aluminum fabrication, glass, and other materials, is a hefty $20 billion in size. This gives the farm plenty of opportunity to grow from its current size. And even though there might be continued weakness in the long run when it comes to the office space, there's plenty of opportunity when it comes to the healthcare industry, education, government agencies, lodging and recreation, airports, and even multifamily residential.

If the tariff situation persists beyond this year, we could see some continued pain. But at some point, that will come to an end. Management mentioned, for instance, that even after mitigation actions, earnings per share would be negatively affected this year by between $0.35 and $0.45. Naturally, if things continue into next year, which is very possible, we could see some additional weakness. That's especially true when you consider that the tariff situation seems to be worsening instead of getting better. So for those who can't handle a bumpy ride, looking elsewhere for potential might make sense.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I can understand why some investors might be pessimistic or cautious. I believe that Apogee Enterprises is most certainly not the best prospect out there. But it's far from a bad one. Despite the troubles the company is facing, and the risks that it faces with broader economic concerns and tariffs, shares are cheap and the long-term outlook should be positive. So if you can handle these temporary weaknesses, which admittedly could last a year or longer, I think that it will pay off. And for that reason, I've decided to keep the company rated a soft "Buy."

data.title: The Rise Of Separately Managed Accounts - Focus On Munis, September 2025
data.description:
By Patricia Healy, CFA

Cumberland has used separately managed accounts (SMAs) to execute its fixed-income strategies since the company’s inception in 1973, long before SMAs were popularized in the early 2000s.

The reasons for managing money in this fashion are the same today as they were then:

  • Transparency (you know what you own)

  • Flexibility to make strategic changes

  • Ability to manage transaction costs and best execution

  • Active management

SMAs offer individually tailored portfolio management options to address client objectives, including tax management, income production, state-specific taxation exemption, cash flow needs, and client-specific customizations. SMAs differ from pooled vehicles like mutual funds and ETFs in that each portfolio can be designed uniquely to a single investor. SMAs also differ from pooled vehicles in terms of (a) transparency, (b) containing stated maturity dates (c) liquidity management within the portfolio, and (d) flexibility for tax management, such as tax loss harvesting or customized state bias weightings.

Growth in SMAs

The advantages of SMAs have led to their increased use by investors. SMA municipal fixed-income assets, both taxable and tax-exempt, have grown from $100 billion in 2008 to an estimated $1.2 trillion of the $4 trillion market at the end of Q1 2025. SMA municipal assets have increased while direct retail and mutual fund assets have declined. Mutual funds, including money market, closed-end funds, and ETFs, totaled $1.14 trillion in muni holdings at the end of Q1 2025. SMA holdings are not separately reported in the Fed data; they are a subset of the household or retail holdings. The Q1 data that we cite is from JPMorgan and was estimated by data derived from SEC Form ADV filings by investment management firms and JPM customer surveys.

Technological improvements have also made it more efficient for managers to offer lower minimums for investing in fixed-income SMAs. At Cumberland our minimum is $250,000 and has been for some time. This size allows us to purchase enough bonds to provide diversification.

ETFs

Exchange-traded fund (ETF) volume has grown almost 73% since 2021 to over $150 billion. Although fast-growing, ETFs constitute only 3% of the $4 trillion municipal market. Conversion of mutual funds to ETFs, as well as organic growth of ETFs, has contributed to the increase. Advantages of ETFs include intraday liquidity, low cost, diversification for smaller investors, and quick exposure to the asset class for larger managers. Some investors have chosen to move into ETFs as a placeholder in anticipation of bigger supply and higher rates before reinvesting in individual bonds or SMAs.

Fund Flows

Mutual fund flows, followed regularly by the market as an indication of demand, are affected by a herd mentality and thus present opportunities for active fixed-income management. Flows into and out of mutual funds can turn very quickly. When investors are dumping assets, the mutual fund portfolio manager may not be able to fully practice active management and must liquidate funds as required by redemptions. Direct retail and SMAs tend to be stickier and, for example, may be able to take advantage of opportunities/yields when the market gets oversold.

Demand, Muni Bond Volume and Value

The muni market has experienced good demand this year even with the substantial increase in issuance. Issuance has grown from $485 billion in 2021 (having dipped to $385 billion for each of 2022 and 2023) to $508 billion in 2024. Volume for 2025 is already surpassing levels from this time last year; and by some estimates, if issuance keeps on track, volume could be $535 billion. The increase in issuance is due to numerous factors, including municipalities retaining funds to increase reserves for unseen events, funding deferred maintenance, building new infrastructure to maintain resiliency, and accommodating the changes in funding to states and projects that the federal government is considering.

Over the past 20 weeks, there have been 18 weeks of inflows into mutual funds and ETFs – an indication of demand likely reflecting higher muni yields and strong ratios. The 10y muni was at 2.93%, and the 10y Treasury was 4.06% for the week ending September 12, which results in muni/Treasury ratios of 71% for the 10y and 90% for the 30y. Depending on an investor’s tax bracket, these levels provide compelling taxable equivalent yields.

More thoughts on muni portfolio management and muni yields can be found in Ben Pease’ July 9th commentary A Forward-Looking Perspective on Municipal Bonds.

The chart below shows that cycles of inflows and outflows have occurred numerous times, and each time the recovery in rates has been quick.

Municipal Bond Credit Quality

Municipal bonds in general have higher ratings, less correlation to corporate bonds, and higher yields than other worldwide offerings do. Moody’s data show that many muni bonds are rated in the Aa category, while corporate bonds are skewed to the lower ratings, including non-investment-grade and many in the Baa category. At Cumberland we invest in taxable munis in our taxable fixed-income strategies, in addition to Treasurys, agencies, and highly rated corporate bonds.

Efficiencies

At Cumberland, we buy bonds in aggregated lots and allocate positions to individual portfolios, a strategy which may allow for better execution and pricing for our clients compared with doing individual trades for each client. When selling for individual-client money-raise requests, we use our extensive network of resources and knowledge of the markets and the particulars of a client’s portfolio to achieve best execution.

Retail accounts do not enjoy the economies of scale that are available to an SMA manager. In addition, active SMA managers that practice total-return investing may have credit-research resources and relationships with many broker dealers that allow them to achieve competitive execution and develop strategies to optimize investment holdings to meet individual clients’ needs.

While mutual fund shares can be purchased and sold any day in any amount, an SMA account has many individual holdings that may take longer to sell. However, when an investor sells shares in a mutual fund, the price received is calculated at the end of the day, based on the net asset value of the fund. If an investor is instead invested in high-quality liquid bonds like the ones Cumberland purchases in its accounts, then, barring an extraordinary event in the market, there should be ample liquidity, and the bonds could be sold at a time that achieves best execution. Additionally, knowing our clients’ needs, Cumberland is looking ahead to provide liquidity when needed. SMAs provide the advantage of customization, allowing clients the ability to determine sectors or categories to be excluded or included for a client’s desired impact, such as no tobacco-related securities or an emphasis on education or water quality. Customization is not possible with a mutual fund or an ETF.

Separately managed accounts generally have higher minimum investment requirements than mutual funds, ETFs, and robo advisors do; so they are not available to all investors. But as an investor acquires more assets and develops more highly tailored goals and objectives, an SMA may be appropriate.

Finally, the management fee charged on SMA accounts can be affected by the competitive environment. The fee is based on the type of strategy and can be scaled based on the level of assets invested. There may also be custodial fees charged to the account. Mutual funds have an expense ratio, which includes a management fee as well as miscellaneous ancillary expenses, custodial expenses, and a distribution charge. Many have various levels of sales charges. So, it is important to look at all expenses when comparing funds.

The Cumberland Approach

At Cumberland, we have a top-down approach to investment management. We look at global macroeconomic conditions and policies to assess interest rates and growth prospects to position our portfolios accordingly. Each market and/or sector is evaluated as to how it fits into the global outlook as well as how its idiosyncratic elements may affect supply and credit quality.

The majority of our fixed-income portfolios are managed on a total-return basis using a barbell strategy to take advantage of changes in interest rate and technical movements more quickly. The use of a barbell strategy allows us to invest in various short-term instruments that are liquid and to use them as ammunition to buy longer-dated bonds when interest rates rise or to take advantage of the higher coupons of longer-maturity bonds compared with shorter-dated bonds. Floating-rate notes and inflation-protected securities are investments that can help returns in the face of inflation and rising interest rates.

Returns of an account are measured against a benchmark, which is usually an index that is widely recognized. Outperformance may mean that individual portfolio returns are less negative than the benchmark’s return or that positive returns are greater than the benchmark’s.

Fixed-income total-return investing takes into consideration price appreciation or depreciation and the effects of coupon income generated and reinvested. Coupon payments over time are a large contributor to the return of an account, but the timing of buying and selling and the choice of where along the curve to buy or sell can greatly impact returns. Other buy-sell considerations include duration, or the sensitivity of a bond to changes in interest rates; embedded options such as call features; supply and demand; coupon structure; and credit-quality trends. All of these can affect the performance of a portfolio relative to an index or benchmark. Finally, to quote Cumberland’s CIO John Mousseau, “Active management means active thinking, not always active trading.”

In summary, we continue to operate as Cumberland’s founders did, investing clients’ funds in separately managed accounts. Our approach to investing is top-down and takes account of global interest rate expectations and credit quality trends as well as characteristics of each bond.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: September BoE: A Pause Amidst "Prominent" Upside Inflation Risks
data.description:
The Bank of England held rates steady in September, extending its pattern of alternating between cuts and pauses while signaling a more unified stance than in recent months. The decision reflects a balance between acknowledging the progress made in bringing inflation lower and maintaining caution as price pressures remain elevated. With growth and employment proving more resilient than expected earlier in the year, the Committee’s attention is increasingly centered on inflation risks, which continue to play a decisive role in shaping policy.

Decision

The Bank of England (BoE) decided to maintain its Bank Rate at 4.00% at the conclusion of the September meeting, continuing the pattern of alternating between cuts and pauses for the 10th straight meeting. The decision was less divided than the early August cut (which barely made it over the line), with 7 members voting for the pause and 2 members voting for a quarter-point cut. This vote was also more decisive than the June pause, which was decided with a 6-3 vote. Overall, the Bank of England’s pause today has the strongest majority since March.

In addition to the pause, the BoE’s Monetary Policy Committee [MPC] voted to reduce the stock of UK government bond purchases held for monetary policy purposes by £70 billion over the next twelve months, bringing the total to £488 billion, down from the total of £558 billion set in the September 2024 meeting. Seven members backed the £70 billion path, while Catherine Mann preferred a slightly smaller £62 billion reduction, and Huw Pill a larger £100 billion reduction, and the Committee reaffirmed that any change to the pace outside the annual review would face a high bar. This decision represents the Bank of England’s continuation of quantitative tightening, with most members aligned on the chosen pace, and only modest debate over whether the process should be somewhat slower or faster, reinforcing the Bank’s preference for a steady and predictable approach.

The focus of the statement given today is inflation, as it appears to be the main thinking keeping the MPC from cutting rates further (coincidentally, this decision was immediately preceded by a UK CPI release). The MPC starts out by pointing out the “substantial disinflation” that has occurred over the past two and a half years and explains that this “progress has allowed for reductions in [the] Bank Rate over the past year.” While inflation has generally fallen, there is still work to be done, with both CPI and core CPI annual inflation above 3.5%. The MPC continues to describe the stickiness in inflation this year as a “temporary increase,” but at the same time, it highlights that “upside risks” to inflationary pressures are a “prominent” part of the outlook.

The general stance on inflation is a bit mixed. The MPC wants to point to a trend of disinflation that “has generally continued,” but it also must voice a concern of upside risks in its economic outlook. This echoes the sentiment in the last few announcements, but interestingly, the assessment of upside inflation risks looks to be getting stronger. Specifically, we can see a difference in how it is referenced in the August statement and today’s statement:

  • August: “The MPC judges that the upside risks around medium-term inflationary pressures have moved slightly higher since May.”
  • September: “Upside risks around medium-term inflationary pressures remain prominent in the Committee’s assessment of the outlook.”

Using the word “prominent” does not feel like an accident. I believe that several members of the MPC have become very vigilant of inflationary pressures and are unlikely to vote for another cut unless inflation starts to come down again.

Why can the BoE move towards a more intense focus on inflation? A large part of it is because trends in growth and the labor market have been more resilient than they were expected to be. In the first half of the year, US tariff policy and global trade uncertainty cast a shadow over the UK economic outlook, but those factors have not been as detrimental as previously thought. Employment gains have actually been pretty solid recently, increasing 232k in the three months to July, and the unemployment rate at 4.7% is a tick below the Bank of England’s Q3 2025 forecast. GDP growth in Q2 2025 surprised to the upside, increasing 0.3% QoQ when growth was expected at 0.1% QoQ. This stronger data suggests that growth and employment are now less of a concern for the MPC than they appeared in Q1 and Q2, which is reflected in the September monetary policy statement, where the Committee devoted just three sentences to the topic.

Market Reaction

The September Bank of England decision was one of the less eventful decisions: the Bank Rate was left unchanged, and the MPC did not update the Monetary Policy Report or its economic outlook. Instead, the pause (and quantitative tightening expansion) and the statement seem to be a continuation of the gradual trend towards hawkishness that has developed as monetary policy has become less restrictive. The market’s reaction was mixed but ultimately reflected that.

  • Equities were bullish in the immediate response to the release but moved towards a more bearish trend as the trading day went on. The FTSE 100 was up around 0.2% two hours after the release, with a peak gain of 0.4% earlier. Modest gains were visible across major indexes with the exception of the riskier FTSE AIM indexes. The FTSE AIM All-Share Index fell -0.1%, and the FTSE AIM 100 index dropped -0.4%.
  • UK gilt yields started off on a dovish footing, dropping in the first 30 minutes following the decision. But the trend flipped to hawkish after that. Towards the end of the trading day, the yield curve steepened, with short-term yields dropping slightly and long-term yields seeing a more substantial rise. The largest moves were in the 10-year and 30-year gilt markets, where yields were up 4 bps in both cases.

With this decision behind us, the next question is whether the Bank of England will continue its pattern with a cut in the upcoming November meeting. Based on the trend in voting and the more intense focus on inflation, it seems like there is a good chance of the pattern breaking. The updates of inflation data over the next few months will be key for investors and policymakers in deciding whether there is any evidence of disinflation that will keep the BoE confident about continuing rate cuts.

data.title: Roche Expands Cardiometabolic Pipeline With 89bio Acquisition
data.description:
Roche Holding AG’s (OTCQX:RHHBY) quest to build a powerful cardiovascular franchise continued this week through external efforts – the acquisition of 89bio, Inc. (NASDAQ:ETNB) for up to $3.5 billion, consisting of a $2.4 billion buyout value and additional $1.1 billion through contingent value rights ("CVRs") within the next 10 years.

The deal value is largely inconsequential for Roche considering its size, but I am in favor of it, as Roche gains pegozafermin as a promising potential treatment for metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis ("MASH") and severe hypertriglyceridemia ("SHTG"), and a potential combination partner for its existing and/or to be acquired product candidates.

I also believe that 89bio investors are getting a good exit value along with long-term upside through the CVRs, because continuing as a standalone company would likely result in additional dilution and with an uncertain outlook considering the evolution of the targeted markets.

Deal overview – will CVRs be paid out?

Roche will acquire 89bio for $14.50 per share or $2.4 billion, and 89bio shareholders will also receive non-tradeable CVRs to receive up to $6.00 per cash or $1.1 billion in aggregate, for a total deal value of up to $3.5 billion.

The terms of the non-tradeable CVRs are:

  • $2.00 per in cash after the first commercial sale of pegozafermin in F4 MASH cirrhotic patients and it expires on March 31, 2030.
  • $1.50 per share in cash if pegozafermin reaches at least $3 billion in annual sales in any calendar year, through the end of 2033.
  • $2.50 per share in cash if pegozafermin reaches at least $4 billion in annual sales in any calendar year, through the end of 2035.

The CVRs show what Roche and 89bio believe is achievable by 2035 and if these kinds of annual sales are realized, Roche would make a substantial return on its investment.

Competitor Madrigal (MDGL) is building the MASH market with Rezdiffra as the first approved drug for the condition, and Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) semaglutide (Wegovy) has recently joined the party last month after receiving FDA approval. Sales of Rezdiffra are off to an excellent start and have reached $212.8 million in Q2, its fifth quarter on the market.

I do believe that a drug like pegozafermin has the potential to generate several billions of dollars in annual sales in the 2030s, but the CVR expiry dates look tight to me, leaving little room for clinical trial hiccups or regulatory delays.

89bio is conducting a phase 3 trial of pegozafermin in F2/F3 (non-cirrhotic) MASH patients with an estimated histology readout in the first half of 2027 and an outcomes phase 3 trial in F4 (cirrhotic) MASH patients with an estimated interim histology readout in 2028.

Based on these timelines, I would assume commercial launches in the second half of 2028 in the U.S. and in 2029 in ex-U.S. territories for the F2/F3 population and a year later for the F4 population. I would also assume increased competition in the 2030s since other companies are actively developing MASH treatments.

One would also have to assume that clinical success is likely, and while I think it is for the non-cirrhotic F2/F3 MASH patients, it is far from guaranteed in the cirrhotic F4 MASH indication, and pegozafermin may not meet the 2-year histology endpoint in 2028 which is required to secure accelerated approval before the expiry date of the first CVR, and it may need to continue to final outcomes analysis that will not be done before the CVR cutoff date as the estimated study completion date is August 2031.

Considering these circumstances, I believe that the probability of these CVRs paying out are below average – around 25-30% for the approval-based CVR due to the clinical risks and timing risks, and 30-35% for the two sales-based CVRs, primarily due to the launch timing risk and the increased competition risk (as mentioned, I do think that the probability of clinical success in F2/F3 MASH is high).

Pegozafermin looks like a competitive MASH and SHTG candidate

Based on the clinical data 89bio generated to date, pegozafermin looks like a good drug candidate for the treatment of MASH.

It looks competitive in F2/F3 non-cirrhotic MASH patients with efficacy near the top of the FGF21 class and near the top of the whole competitive field on the relevant endpoint of placebo-adjusted one or more stage improvement in fibrosis and no worsening of MASH.

The obvious caveat here are that these are cross-trial comparisons and that none of these candidates were tested against each other.

Pegozafermin also worked well on other relevant endpoints for MASH in the phase 2b trial:

  • MASH resolution without worsening of fibrosis. At week 24, the response rates were between 23% and 37% versus 2% for placebo.
  • Response rates were between 14% and 20% for pegozafermin versus 0% for placebo on the combined endpoint of MASH resolution and fibrosis improvement.
  • 50-55% liver fat reductions versus 15% in the placebo group.
  • Markers of liver damage and inflammation (AST/ALT) also substantially declined with nearly two thirds of patients achieving ALT normalization.
  • Pegozafermin also showed positive biomarker effects in patients already on GLP-1 agonists like semaglutide.

Importantly, pegozafermin was generally safe and well-tolerated, indicating a good risk-benefit as a potential treatment for MASH.

The F2/F3 MASH market is substantial and is expected to grow at a compounded rate of either 3% with incretin drugs (like semaglutide and tirzepatide) or 5% without them, and it is expected to exceed 10 million patients in the U.S. alone by 2035. 89bio further estimates that the diagnosed patient pool will more than triple from approximately 570,000 patients to more than 1.8 million.

The F4 MASH population is smaller, but also very significant with an estimated prevalence of 3.6 million to 4.2 million patients and the estimated eligible pool of diagnosed patients of approximately 820,000 by 2035.

This translates to a combined market size of approximately $90 billion in 2035 (based on the combined estimated diagnosed population of 2.6 million patients) if we take Rezdiffra’s estimated net price per patient per year of approximately $35,000 (my estimate based on quarterly patient numbers and net sales of Rezdiffra shared by Madrigal).

Combination treatments are likely the future in this market and this is where the difficulties would start for 89bio and where Roche is much better positioned to continue the development and commercialization of pegozafermin.

Competitor Madrigal has recently in-licensed a preclinical oral GLP-1 candidate which it intends to combine with Rezdiffra for the treatment of MASH patients, and Roche already has and will continue to build a robust portfolio of cardiometabolic products and candidates, and it more than likely already has combination approaches in mind with its existing obesity candidates.

I should also mention the other shot on goal for pegozafermin – severe hypertriglyceridemia ("SHTG"), which is another sizable market, and pegozafermin has shown it can significantly reduce triglycerides. Phase 3 results are expected in the first quarter of 2026.

Severe hyperglyceridemia is a market that has not seen a lot of innovation until very recently. Ionis Pharmaceuticals (IONS) has reported very strong results of olezarsen (a drug that reduces APOC3 gene expression) at the start of the month with substantial triglyceride reductions and significant reductions in the incidence of acute pancreatitis versus placebo. Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR) is close behind with its siRNA drug plozasiran that also reduces APOC3 and which I expect to report similarly strong phase 3 results in mid-2026.

89bio is conducting a single and relatively small phase 3 study of pegozafermin in patients with severe hypertriglyceridemia, and the trial is not powered for acute pancreatitis reductions and the regulatory requirements also call for a much larger safety database and a second phase 3 trial. As such, this is a program that could result in an approved indication behind one or both MASH indications, and it could help hit those second and third CVR sales targets by the end of 2033 and 2035.

However, we have to see the data first and whether Roche plans to continue to pursue this indication.

Conclusion

Roche continues its ambitious efforts to build a cardiometabolic franchise that should power the growth of the company in the 2030s. The acquisition of 89bio brings pegozafermin as a potential treatment of MASH, a rapidly growing and increasingly validated commercial market for biotech companies, and potentially for SHTG.

Roche is much better suited to bring pegozafermin to market and to push the program forward by combining it with its current or to-be-acquired product candidates.

This also looks like a decent exit for 89bio shareholders considering the circumstances – a large buyout premium and the elimination of the risks of continuing as a standalone company, and those risks are primarily dilution, clinical trial risks, increasing competition, and the need to come up with combination treatments.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ECNS: A Flawed Product, But Some Mitigating Reasons To Consider
data.description:
ETF Profile

The iShares MSCI China Small-Cap ETF (NYSEARCA:ECNS), is a $121M-sized (by AUM) ETF that has been offering coverage to small listed companies in China since September 2010. ECNS, which currently covers 262 stocks, seeks to achieve its objective by passively tracking the MSCI China Small Cap Index [MCSCI] by way of a sampling process (what this means is that ECNS doesn’t necessarily own all the stocks from this index, but only a sample of stocks- typically 80% similarity- which jointly mirror the qualities of the index).

It’s worth noting that the broad Chinese equity universe is captured by the MSCI China All Shares Index, and while the top 85% of this (by way of free-float market-capitalization) is represented by the famed MSCI China Index (widely perceived to be the prime Chinese benchmark by the institutional investor community), the bottom 14% of the Chinese equity universe is captured by MCSCI.

Like the MSCI China Index, MCSCI too is dominated by P-Chip Chinese stocks, which account for 57% of the total index. In case you remain oblivious to this terminology, do note that P-Chips are essentially non-state-owned Chinese stocks that are incorporated outside China, and listed on the Hong Kong stock exchange, but derive over 55% of their revenue from domestic China.

A Few Concerns

It appears that ECNS doesn’t do a very good job of tracking MCSCI, as the tracking errors across different durations are of a very high level of 25% (most passive ETFs witness tracking errors within the single digit mark)

ECNS isn’t also a particularly stable portfolio, with typically over 60% of its portfolio getting turned over on an annual basis (for context, most ETFs witness annual churn of roughly half of ECNS’s pace).

In addition to that, as you would imagine, small-caps also tend to exhibit a lot more volatility than their large-cap brethren. Take note of ECNS’s annual volatility, which is quite concerning at close to 37% (this is over twice as much as what a standard ETF experiences). ECNS is also prone to attracting the interest of short sellers which currently account for 25% of the total shares outstanding.

Investors who pursue emerging market stocks and small cap stocks typically do so because of their relatively rosy prospects of witnessing a high growth runway. It is questionable if ECNS will provide you with these qualities when roughly one-fourth of its holdings consists of healthcare stocks, more than twice as much as the next largest sector. Prima facie, healthcare in innately seen as a low-growth, steady business. Also, for an ETF that tilts to a sector that is perceived to be a defensive play, the overall volatility profile is quite at odds.

If there’s a redeeming aspect of ECNS, it is perhaps its dividend profile (distributions are made semi-annually), which has largely worked out to a +4% annualized figure over the last decade. However the current figure of 4.16% is lower than the 10-year average of 4.3%.

How Has ECNS ETF Performed?

To get a broader sense of ECNS’s performance over time we thought it would be relevant to juxtapose it against large and mid-cap Chinese stocks (as represented by MCHI), and emerging market small-cap stocks (as represented by EEMS). As you can see from the image below, on a total return basis, the performance has been quite underwhelming, with ECNS generating returns of only 57% since August 2011 (when EEMS, the ETF with the most recent listing date of these 3 got listed). In contrast, small-caps from other EMs have surged ahead by 1.81x, while large and mid-cap Chinese stocks too have outperformed by 1.64x!

Total returns may not necessarily provide a comprehensive perspective of things as it doesn’t take into consideration the degree of risk these products have encountered to facilitate those returns. If you’re looking for insight in that regard, one may take a look at the long and short-term Sharpe ratios of these respective products. This metric measures the excess returns of these products (relative to the risk-free rate) as a function of their respective standard deviation profiles. The image below shows that be it over 10 years or the most recent 3 years, ECNS’s Sharpe ratios have always lagged the ratios of the other two products.

Supplementing the Sharpe, we also have pretty underwhelming readings on the Sortino ratio front (it is similar to the Sharpe except that it only looks at the downside deviation of these products rather than total deviation) with ECNS coming last yet again regardless of the time frame.

Closing Thoughts- Some Mitigating Reasons To Pursue ECNS

Hitherto what we have written so far about ECNS doesn’t necessarily paint this product in good light. With the exception of a +4% yield, we haven’t been able to come across too many compelling reasons to pursue ECNS. As a result, we find it difficult to recommend this product.

However, one additional factor that may help drum up some interest in ECNS is its relatively cheap valuation quotient.

ECNS can be picked up at an earnings multiple of 12.7x, which represents a 12-14% discount over Chinese large-caps and emerging market small-caps. Meanwhile, we acknowledge, the cash flow multiple differential with EM small caps is not huge, but relative to Chinese large-caps it represents a significant discount of 40%!

In light of the substantial valuation gap with Chinese large-caps, we can see investors who also appreciate the theme of mean-reversion gravitating towards ECNS at this juncture. Note that the Chinese small-cap to large-cap ratio is currently 24% lower than its long-term mean of 0.785, offering some scope of upward normalization.

Also note that ECNS may appeal to momentum players, as it continues to witness a series of higher-highs and higher-lows and is now trading above all its key moving averages on the daily chart.

data.title: Nebius Group: Global Infrastructure Expansion To Drive Growth And Value
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

In my previous article, I focused on Nebius Group’s (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:NBIS) partnership with NVIDIA, and I still believe this partnership continues to provide leverage for Nebius Group’s upside potential. The company has gained about 19% since my previous article, and this is aproximately one-fifth of my last 100% upside potential.

In this article, I am rating Nebius Group as a buy driven by a multi-billion-dollar commitment to scale its AI infrastructure with Microsoft. This deal is intertwined with its core business in AI cloud, which I highlighted in my previous article, as this is the primary ground through which Nebius will accelerate its AI cloud business from 2026 and beyond. In this global infrastructure expansion, Nebius is moving its focus to the European market, to the global AI cloud market that has a CAGR of 28.5%. Therefore, Nebius is quadrupling data center capacity by 2026 to tap into this rapidly growing AI cloud market, and securing well-located and high-quality land plots with access to reliable providers in the United States is part of this long-term goal. The purchase of plots is an execution of Nebius’ forecast to reach a 100MW data center in its own land before the end of 2025, and as a chance to expand to 300 MW as cloud AI demand continues soaring. Let me delve into details to show what this means in terms of this AI cloud opportunity in my explanations below, also showing why I am reiterating my previous bullish rating.

The Global Infrastructure Perspective

On a global scale, Cloud AI has the potential to grow from $102.09 billion in 2025 to $589.22 billion in 2032 at a CAGR of 28.5%.

The sector is gaining a high adoption rate of 94% of enterprises globally using cloud computing. This has increased workloads to 72% compared to 66% in FY2024. The primary driver of high adoption, according to 78% of IT decision-makers, is the development of infrastructure. North America is leading in terms of cloud AI with a market share of 39% which shows a huge potential due to high adoption rates. In fact, over 88% of enterprises are actively investing 5% of their expenditure on AI plans. 54% of global enterprises plan to allocate over 25% of their spending on AI plans.

While global enterprises are adopting cloud AI at 94%, deeper evaluation shows that out of this high adoption rate, AWS leads globally in terms of cloud adoption with its cloud infrastructure at 31%, seconded by Microsoft Azure at 25% and Google Cloud follows at 11%. If you compare the 94% enterprise adoption and the leading cloud adoption rate in infrastructure at 31%, this is a vast gap currently, which is approximately 60%. This shows how much the sector is yet to be fully tapped, which forms a basis that I think Nebius’ global expansion, especially in the US, is a significant growth driver.

When I take the highest hyperscaler cloud infrastructure at 31% and compare that with the adoption rate of 94%, I think that if the cloud infrastructure is already there, it means the infrastructure would be closely, if not all, utilised. Why do I think this way? Let’s look at it this way. Today, in 2025, the total workload, both AI workload and non-AI workload, is 82 GW. AI workload is currently at 44 GW and non-AI workload at 38 GW. Non-AI workload is not increasing compared to AI workload because from 44 GW in 2025, AI workload is estimated to grow approximately 4 times from the current to 156 GW by 2030, while non-AI workload is expected to grow to 64 GW in 2030 from 38GW today.

This deeper angle of looking at AI shows that the AI workload is already complex and is overwhelming the existing on-premise infrastructure. It is this mismatch that is driving the cloud-based AI solutions, which makes AI cloud the best alternative for enterprises like hyperscalers looking for scale and affordability. AI cloud adoption demonstrated by the hyperscalers’ adoption above brings an up-close picture of why AI growth is at an infant stage, and therefore, the best stage to get in.

Nebius Seizing The Opportunity

When I go back to Nebius, I note that this global expansion to the US, like securing plots for more data center installations to scale its cloud AI, is a major a good initiative to maximize on the market Opportunity. For instance, following the partnership with Microsoft worth $19.4 billion, Nebius is set to receive over $17.4 billion in the next five years for its data center in New Jersey.

Given that Nebius has already committed to capital raising efforts such as convertible notes and IPO raising over $4.2 billion, this shows how far the sector is from hype to a modern-day gold rush. My explanation of the US expansion is part of the Nebius commitment to operate in the other seven AI clusters in six different countries across Europe and the Middle East. Following the partnership with NVIDIA, Nebius is leveraging this association to deploy thousands of NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra GPUS across these expansion areas and expects those deployed in the UK to be operational in Q4’2025.

This expansion is entrenched in Nebius Group’s core investment proposition in its ability to scale AI infrastructure in response to the surging global demand. As such, this expansion is a plan that will upgrade revenue targets further as AI technologies continue to spur its growth.

Revisiting Valuation

In my previous article, I had estimated the price target for the company at $137.66 with an upside potential of about 100% by 2026 based on a P/S based model. Given the global expansion, I am reviewing my price estimation because I expect revenue growth to accelerate which could also push its P/S upward. Before I update my financial estimates, I will draw your attention to the company’s revenue revisions where by it has seen 4 upwards revisions. This has pushed its consensus revenue by 2026 to $1.54 billion up from $1.39 billion as at my previous publication. This indeed shows that the market is appreciating this company’s growth catalyst which aligns with my bullish take.

Given this background, I will also review my financial estimates. For 2025, my revise my growth rate from 300% to about 350% and my 2026 revenue growth from 250% to 270% to reflect on the potential impact of the global expansion the company is undertaking. I will maintain my profit margins at 70% and 76% respectively for 2025 and 2026 FY. For my P/S ratio, I had previously used a forward P/S of 20x but I believe with the new catalysts, the market sentiments could improve alongside improving revenue hence attracting a higher forward P/S. For this case, I will assume 22x forward P/S. With the revised assumptions, I am projecting revenue of about $1.9 billion by 2026 which is close to the consensus $1.54 billion.

Using this projected revenue and a forward P/S of 22x, I am estimating a price target of $176.3 which translates to an upside potential of about 97%. For this reason, I believe this is a buy.

Investment Risks

Dependence on NVIDIA – The technological dependence on NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs in its expansion activities is a risky position that can reduce Nebius’ competitiveness through the development of its own proprietary AI chips.

Limited differentiation of hardware– reliance on NVIDIA’s latest chips faces a risk of larger cloud providers like Google Cloud accessing a similar service level. The only differentiation Nebius has is in superior customer service, like discounts and niche expertise. If the relationship with NVIDIA falls short, it may affect the company’s access to chips that present its hardware advantage.

Conclusion

I am reiterating my bullish stance on Nebius Group, and this time, the global extension drives this bullish move. After raising $4.0 billion to finance the New Jersey data center in the US, this goes beyond to show the company’s commitment to executing the deal. This opens a window for beginning to record revenues from the $17.5 billion in the next 5 years. Coupled with other global extensions, the company’s revenues are set to soar further as discussed above, thus unlocking value.

data.title: You Can Beat The Market - By Breaking Some Rules
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

David Gardner, co-founder of The Motley Fool, author of Rule Breaker Investing, shares why it's possible to beat the market (0:30). Investing is not just a math exercise (5:00). Starbucks stock and playing the long game (8:20). Investing principles that have led to wins (12:40). Management is key (19:00). Overvalued as a buy signal (26:20). Parallels to dotcom era (33:45).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Very happy to welcome to the show David Gardner, co-founder of The Motley Fool, author of the fantastic new book, Rule Breaker Investing. David, so happy to have you on Seeking Alpha. So happy to have you on Investing Experts. Thanks for coming on.

David Gardner: Well, thank you very much for the invitation, Rena. I'm really looking forward to the conversation.

RS: Likewise. You know, The Motley Fool and Seeking Alpha both really at the forefront of helping retail investors, of helping investors secure the best alpha, secure the best profit.

Also, we are in the midst, as everyone seems to be aware of, of many, many paradigm shifts happening in the world at large, in the economy, in the stock market.

You just wrote a book, Rule Breaker Investing. What would you say right now as investors try and understand this perhaps new iteration of the markets, what is best to keep in mind these days, would you say?

DG: Well, I think first of all, I've always loved your name Seeking Alpha. I also like Rena. That's actually my daughter-in-law's name. But I was gonna call out Seeking Alpha because I think that's what we should be doing as investors.

I really believe that it is not just not luck to beat the market. I believe it's very possible to beat the market, and I believe that I have done that over thirty years now.

And so the purpose of Rule Breaker Investing is to basically leave it all out there on the field. This is my last stock market book, and I try to put into it everything that I use to beat the market.

To answer your question, I think the first thing upfront is the time frame that we're gonna take to our investing. I feel very strongly that you should have a whole life time frame, not a not a three year time frame, not a ten year time frame.

I think you should be invested your whole life and just keep buying and dollar cost averaging into the best individual stocks you can find.

Of course, for years of The Motley Fool, we've said index funds are great, and they are for a lot of Americans who just don't wanna spend time listening to us on a podcast.

Index funds are a wonderful answer. But I suspect because of the audience we have listening to us today, they're interested in beating the market. And for me, the best way to do that is to find excellence, buy excellence, and add to excellence over time and sell mediocrity, and time is the key player.

So those are a few I realized, yeah, there are some paradigm resets and other things happening in society. The oncoming AI reminds me of the dawn of the Internet. There's a lot of analogies I see thirty years apart between these things.

And it was always the right move thirty years ago to be playing the long game as an investor and buying great stocks, and that's gonna be true over the next thirty years as well.

RS: Do you also believe that part of the portfolio is meant to be played with - less about more certain picks towards excellence and more guesses at excellence?

DG: I really appreciate that question. I would say, my principle number one of the rule breaker portfolio right there at the start of part three is make your portfolio reflect your best vision for our future.

So as you might imagine, I'm a huge fan of people making their portfolios fun for them, personal. I feel like I should be able to look up and down your brokerage statement, and I should know you better because the companies that you're invested in should really say a lot about you in the same way our books say a lot about us.

What's on our shelf says a lot about what's in our brain. I feel very strongly the same should be true of your your portfolio. So, yeah, I think it's fine to have some speculative aspects. I think we should always be learning, always pushing out the boundaries a little bit more.

I would keep almost any stock that I bought, I don't think I'm right. I'm not ever positive. I don't go in with this high confidence. That's why I really believe you should start with a portfolio of 20 stocks at a minimum, and then let's see what horses start getting ahead down the track and then invest accordingly.

And one thing I love about the stock market more so than horse betting is you can bet all the way through the race. So as Secretariat starts surging out front, you can bet on Secretariat then. You don't have to just guess ahead of time. So I think it's very helpful to know the things and observe the world that you're investing in. But I also believe that it's helpful to always be trying to learn a little bit more.

So five to 10% of a portfolio could be merely speculative, and it shouldn't just be too crazy. I think it should be to learn and have fun.

RS: Not enough Secretariat references these days, David, really appreciate that.

DG: Let's go.

RS: Let's go. I'm curious. You know, as a co-founder of Motley Fool, as a writer, what have been some of your rules that you've had to break along the way? What has been most salient in that journey for you?

DG: I think first of all, as an investor, I started with the idea that investing was mainly a math exercise. And I was raised to enjoy math. I was actually an English literature major, but I've always loved baseball statistics, and I love sports and stats. So I'm very numerical.

And my dad who taught me investing, taught me how to value line. That big black tome with tons of numbers. It's still around as a business. It's not as big as it once was because the internet has shown up in the meantime.

But I had to disabuse myself of all of the mathematical habits that I'd gotten into thinking that that's the way to invest. And of course, that's important. But the qualitative side of investing is I think really where I buttered my bread as an investor a lot in my book, Rule Breaker Investing, is about the qualitative things, the things where there are no numbers.

One of my key lines in the book is there are no numbers for the things that matter most. And, for example, who the CEO of the company is matters hugely to your investment returns. There is no line item anywhere in the financial statements for the plus or minus value of that person.

So it's like watching the NFL trying to guess what's gonna be a good team, but not being allowed to know what the quarterbacks are of any of the teams. That's how people are investing if they're making it pure math without looking at the qualitative inputs that determine the outputs of cash flow and earnings.

So that would be a a really good example, I think, for me as a younger investor. You know, starting The Motley Fool, we had a lot of people in my twenties and thirties relying on the advice that my brother Tom and I were putting out through our website. People paying us for our next stock pick.

And so you learn in an accelerated fashion. You need to learn what's gonna win and work when you have a lot of people following you. So that was an incredibly helpful lesson. And I've learned a lot more lessons.

I'll just stir one more thing in the cauldron here, as an entrepreneur myself. And so you know, Rena, working within a business and I know you've been at Seeking Alpha for around seventeen years. You know that you get such insights working within a business yourself and then you can translate those into the stock picks and analysis that you do of other people's companies, which is what we do when we pick stocks.

So my favorite Warren Buffett line and I have very mixed feelings about Warren Buffett. I I'm a huge fan of his character, but I have a very different approach to beating the market.

But one of my favorite lines from Buffett is, I'm a better investor because I'm a businessman and a better businessman because I'm an investor.

And I really believe those things are integrally connected. And I didn't understand that when I first started investing in stocks at the age of 18.

RS: Yeah. And I would say I did not understand that seventeen years ago as I came on board at Seeking Alpha at the dawn of the great financial crisis. There was a lot to learn about the world and how intertwined everything is, especially in the business world and in the financial world and in the stock market world.

You mentioned the story at the outset of your book about going on The View and trying to bring in stock investing to the masses and how that can be a difficult errand because of people's desire for quick returns, instant gratification. It's hard to play the long game in investing.

I'm curious what has strengthened your conviction, or what has helped you encourage investors to strengthen their conviction? And I think part and parcel of what we've been talking about so far is the the importance of having your own strategy work for you. You have to be okay with whatever your strategy is. Maybe kind of if you can stir all that in the same cauldron and and and I'm interested to hear what you have to say about that.

DG: Well, first of all, just about The View. We picked a stock in 1998, and then we were invited back six weeks later just to update the story on the ABC daytime show. And the stock had lost 33% of its value in just six weeks, which obviously was a surprise to us. And they were good natured about it, but we were never invited back - not a year later or, let's say, twenty seven years later to update the story.

The stock that we picked for the view viewers was Starbucks (SBUX). Starbucks had a bad few weeks that summer when CEO Howard Schultz basically underpromised and would later over deliver future earnings estimates. And so that dumped the stock in the six weeks since we picked it on ABC.

Starbucks is up 34 times in value from the day we first appeared on on ABC. So it's a reminder, obviously, what game are we playing. And I would say it's actually not that hard to play the long game.

I think a lot of people watching us right now, especially people who've been active as investors their whole lives or are in investment clubs, a lot of us, I think, can appreciate and I hope have a long term mentality around our money.

I do think it's hard to do on TV. So I don't think CNBC, I don't spend any time watching CNBC. I think so much media coverage of our markets forces a short term viewpoint. And while ABC's The View is not a stock market investing show, it's sort of a television show.

That's why I've always loved the Internet because the Internet has a memory and you can score things, tag, date stamp things, and check back later. And so I've always been an Internet junkie. But your question was also about conviction and how you develop that and how you grow that over time.

And I think, first of all, as a fool, I never go in... I always come from a place of humility. So as I mentioned earlier, any stock that I recommend or buy, I'm saying I think I'm right. But I don't ever know I'm right. And in fact, one of my, key habits as an investor, as a rule breaker investor is I only add to to winning positions.

I never have added to a losing position. And I realize a lot of people who would call themselves value investors or big Buffett fans might think that's crazy because they're like, hey, if you liked it at 27, surely you like it at 17.

And my answer is not for the kinds of stocks that I recommend that I found over thirty years. Often, they don't really come back in a reliable way.

So I've grown conviction around my style of investing, but we can easily see somebody else who would grow conviction as that stock drops from 27 to 17 that it's an even better buy. And certainly for some companies, that style works.

I think part of it is, what color is your parachute? What is your style of investing? And I think part of the challenge of life is know thyself. That's why it was scrawled on the Greek oracle at Delphi, know thyself.

We're all trying to figure ourselves out over the course of our lives, and we try to get better at that. And as you do, you should let that determine how you invest your money.

So I have a very specific brand of investing that I've sort of self styled and built over thirty years. Happy to say I've found some of the great stocks you could have found, and I bought them early, and I'm still holding them over those thirty years. So that's part and parcel to my strategy, but I realized there are many others, and I hope I did a decent job speaking to that.

RS: I think so. If you could speak a little in more granular detail about your strategy because to your point about when to buy and when to sell and the price, and valuation is such a question mark these days.

It's like, what price is at what stock and why? And is it divorced completely from reality? And so I'm curious to know the metrics that you're basing your strategy on and and various factors like that.

DG: You bet. Well, I have six traits that I look for in stocks and specifically in rule breaker stocks. The more check boxes I can give to the six traits from a single company, the happier I am to to invest in it.

And I'll just run through them really fast because and if we wanna dip into some of them, we can. But here they are in order.

Trade number one is top dog and first mover in an important emerging industry. That always has me focused on the innovators, that has me focused on the upstarts, that has me focused on the Davids that are trying to take down the Goliaths.

And that's why I've managed to find Amazon.com (AMZN) at my cost basis, thanks to stock splits, which is now 16¢, which I've held since 1997. And it also had me recommending, to Motley Fool members, NVIDIA (NVDA) in 2005. Finding it early, still holding it now twenty years later. And my cost basis, thanks to stock splits, is coincidentally 16¢.

So both of those companies are iconically great companies and have ended up being, I think, the best stocks of this era, and they both conform to that first trait of rule breaker investing.

They also conform to the other five. Here they come. Number two, we're looking for companies with sustainable competitive advantage that can be measured in different ways. Some of it is left brained, some of it is right brained.

We can talk more about that. But because I invest for at least three years, sustainable, competitive advantage is critical to my style of investing. Number three, this is the first one that's not about the company. It's about the stock.

Trait number three, this one's contrary. Stellar past price appreciation. I want the stock already to have done well. In a world where people are trying to buy low, sell high, they're waiting for dips, I'm doing the opposite.

We can talk more about that. So those are the first three. The second three, numbers four, five, and six. Number four is good management and smart backing.

The more I've lived, the more I realize the importance of who's running the company. As it turns out, Jeff Bezos had a cost basis of 16¢ while he looked young and seemed like a crazy man. Barron's said amazon.bomb in 2001. His value to Amazon and to investors and Amazon was incalculably high.

And that's really important to recognize. So I'm a huge fan. I'm basically a humanist, small h, because I really think you need to find greatness in people and be invested there in a world where many people don't even care who the CEO is. They're looking at zigs and zags or stock analytics, stock charts. So that's number four.

Number five, strong consumer appeal. I'm a huge fan of branding and great brands. I think those are great moats, competitive advantages under themselves, but I also love the brand isn't measured in the financial statements.

And so every great company looks overvalued because it has a great brand, and no one's counting that in their, price to earnings ratios.

And then the final attribute, the final trait we're looking for in our six traits of the rule breaker stock is that the company be broadly perceived as overvalued. And we can talk more about why I actually think that works even though you'd think it would be the opposite of what you'd wanna do.

So those six traits, something I love about them are they're obviously a main feature of this book. But I first wrote them down in our 1999 Motley Fool book, Rule Breakers, Rule Makers.

The difference between me then and me now is that then I was in my early thirties saying, here's what I think works. This is what I'm doing. I'm putting it out there. And now here I am twenty seven years later going, I'm still using the same six and they have worked wonderfully.

And I believe they will work in every era. And I think people should know these, not for every stock. Because these don't really speak to cyclicals. These are never gonna speak to meme stocks, which I have no interest in.

And they don't speak to lots of other types of companies on the market. After all, how many companies truly are top dog and first mover in an important emerging industry?

The answer is all the best ones. And even the ones that don't work out, I'm fine with because I have more of a VC mentality where as a venture capitalist, I'm willing to lose.

But I'm not a VC at series a or angel investor. I'm a VC for public market stocks, and I hold them for long periods of time.

RS: There's a few things that I would love to pick out. First, I'm curious, on one foot, how many stocks would you say fits under that rubric?

DG: It's a great question. In a lot of ways, the question is how many industries are there? And I tend not to really care about traditional industry designations.

So if I see someone screening for stocks and they've got tech stocks as one of their their industries, that's a phrase that's never meant anything to me.

My first appearances on CNBC in my twenties, I was like, what is a tech stock? Could somebody please define what that is for me? It's never had any meaning to me.

RS: I would say it's gotten less meaningful as the years have gotten.

DG: Yeah. And I appreciate that; I agree with that.

So how we define what an industry is itself sort of a creative choice. And therefore, I don't know that there's a single number we could put on it, but I would say that, I would be open like, generally, the stocked pond of the fish, each of which is a top fish and first mover in an important emerging industry, probably comes down to somewhere between two hundred and five hundred stocks, which may sound like more than people would expect, except that I think that how we classify industries is very interesting, and we can be more interesting in our classification.

So that's roughly how many stocks I'd be looking at.

RS: I would say the sectors is another thing that we could say is shifting its paradigm for sure all over the place.

The other thing I was curious about, we mentioned Starbucks. You mentioned Amazon, two great examples of managers to value and and be impressed by.

In terms of that as a factor, something you're looking at, is there something we had, an analyst, Jack Bowman, on a couple months ago talking about how Amazon - most people think that its moneymaker is Prime, but really it's AWS and Prime is a money loser.

Are there steps along the way or choices along the way or choices not made along the way where you're like, wait a second. He has great background. He has great history. He's done a great job other places. But I'm really questioning or is that the point where you rely on the other factors to kind of bolster your case?

DG: A fun question. And I will just say, first of all, there's no way to nail the answer to that question because it's sort of open ended.

And that's that would be my point is that, part of what I love about caring deeply about who's running the company is we can never really fully know these people.

I mean, even somebody like Elon Musk who is about as overexposed as any business leader in history, he's still largely a mystery to many people. With that said, I've never been an Elon fanboy, but I'm definitely not an Elon hater.

And it seems like the world is divided into those two groups. I'm right in the middle. Actually, I would say I lean toward fanboy because I think there have been few greater value creators, I won't just say living today, I might even say in business history when you look at the scale at which he's achieved innovation and business gains across many different disciplines or industries.

So I think that's a great example. But, you know, does he have a background that would allow him to really run Neuralink or really know what he's doing with brain computer interfaces, I don't exactly know.

But that we're asking that question shows that we're barking up the right tree, I think, because these are the questions that will lead to the actual great winning stocks or great losing stocks of the next generation.

Many people think it's all about waiting for a dip to hit their valuation model for a certain company. And while I certainly respect valuation, they're very short term, the valuations people do.
